title: Multi-hypothesis coordinate regression replaces the 1024-cell field
hypothesis: The champion's field/decode mechanism (32x32-cell classification field -> beta-sharpened soft-argmax) has been completely unchanged since round 16 -- twenty rounds (17-36) have only churned satellite modules around it (samplers, auxiliary losses, gates, a margin hinge) while the frozen core keeps producing the exact failure mode diagnosed in runs/_probe_iter12_field.py and recorded in project memory 'champion-failure-mode-probes': the true cell is the field's top-1 pick on only ~2% of eval crops, yet lands in the field's own top-20 (of 1024) on 33-58% of crops in every lighting bucket. That gap means the descriptor already carries enough signal to narrow the answer down to a small neighborhood -- the failure is specifically that a 1024-way ranking problem is too hard for the head to solve, not that the features are uninformative. Round 34's and round 36's margin-hinge losses attacked this by pushing individual impostor cells down within the SAME 1024-way field and both failed to beat the champion (1e9 gated-fail, then 754.22 vs 743.07) -- evidence the bottleneck is the discretization itself, not which cells get extra gradient. Shrinking the ranking problem from 1-of-1024 fixed grid cells to 1-of-8 data-dependent candidate points should make correct-hypothesis selection tractable while a small K also sidesteps exp 5's diagnosed sparse-supervision problem (a rigid 1024-cell grid needs many training locations per cell to learn anything; 8 free-floating points just need to drift toward wherever they're useful, no fixed-cell occupancy requirement).
method: In model/model.py, delete the 1024-way classification field entirely (loc_logits, dark_logits, gate, cell_u/cell_v grid, GRID_K, DECODE_BETA, the beta-sharpened soft-argmax decode) and replace it with a K=8 multi-hypothesis regression head: a small MLP reads [GAP descriptor, layout code, raw brightness] and outputs 8 candidate (u,v) points (each an offset added to one of 8 fixed, non-trainable anchor positions spread across the map, purely to break symmetry at init) plus 8 softmax mixture weights. Each of the 4 C4-rotation views independently picks its own highest-weight hypothesis; the 4 views' winning points are blended into the final (u,v) by a softmax over their own weights (replacing the old logit-averaging C4 vote with a point-averaging C4 vote). Confidence is rebuilt from the spread of the 4 views' winning points (tight agreement = confident) and their mean weight, replacing the old field-peak/entropy signal. Training signal changes from Gaussian-smoothed 1024-way cross-entropy + coordinate L2 to a winner-take-all regression loss (only the hypothesis nearest the true (u,v) gets the full L2 gradient each step; the other 7 get an epsilon=0.05 trickle to avoid dead hypotheses) plus an 8-way cross-entropy that trains the weight head to identify which hypothesis was nearest. The exp-35 confusability-weighted sampler (Training data, kept, unchanged) still needs a 32x32 grid to define 'look-alike cells' for oversampling -- since model.py no longer has any grid, that constant moves into model/train.py as a standalone CONFUSABILITY_GRID_K, with identical value and math, so the sampler's actual behavior is byte-for-byte unchanged.
expected_outcome: Worst-case median error improves from the champion's 743.07 m to roughly 450-650 m, on the reasoning that an 8-way ranking problem should be far more tractable than the diagnosed 1024-way one given the descriptor already narrows the true location into a small candidate set most of the time. Genuine risk: pure point regression is exactly what exp 1/2 (the project's very first rounds) showed loses badly to a probability-field approach (3216.74 m vs 2489.03 m) because a single regressed point collapses toward the conditional mean on ambiguous/multimodal crops -- this experiment is a bet that K=8 *learned, data-dependent* candidate points with winner-take-all training avoids that collapse (each hypothesis can specialize and only the closest one is graded, so ambiguity is represented by disagreement among the 8 points rather than smeared into one shrunken average), unlike the single-point regression that round 2 correctly moved away from. If this round regresses toward round-1/2 territory instead of improving, that is informative: it means the shrinkage failure mode is about point-estimate training generally, not specifically about the fixed grid, and would argue for reintroducing an explicit multimodal representation (e.g. mixture density output kept for uncertainty at inference) rather than any point-based decode.
eli5: For twenty-some rounds straight, the model has guessed your location the same way: score 1,024 fixed map tiles and pick whichever one scores highest -- like a multiple-choice test with 1,024 options. We measured that the right tile is in the model's own top-20 guesses most of the time, but wins outright only 2% of the time -- so the model roughly knows the neighborhood but keeps losing the final vote to a lookalike tile. This round throws out the fixed 1,024-tile menu and lets the model instead point to 8 of its own candidate spots anywhere on the map, then simply picks whichever one it's most sure about -- like narrowing that multiple-choice test from 1,024 options down to 8, which should be a much easier pick. Each of the four camera headings picks its own best spot, and the four picks get blended by how confident each heading was.

stages.name: Camera frame
stages.detail: 128×128 px aerial crop, one of 6 simulated lighting conditions

stages.name: Rotation fan
stages.detail: the crop is copied into its four 90° turns (exact pixel re-arrangements, no blur) and all four copies travel through the same network side by side

stages.name: Feature extractor
stages.detail: ImageNet-pretrained MobileNetV3-Small (first 9 blocks, 190k params), gently fine-tuned

stages.name: Layout summary
stages.detail: a slim extra layer condenses the 8×8 feature grid into a 512-number code that remembers what sits where in the crop; the texture average (48 numbers) is kept alongside

stages.name: Multi-hypothesis head
stages.detail: a small network reads the layout code, texture average, and the crop's own brightness (no separate day/night expert anymore -- brightness is just one more input number) and proposes 8 candidate map points plus a confidence weight for each, instead of scoring 1,024 fixed map tiles

stages.name: 4-view weighted vote
stages.detail: each of the four rotated copies picks its own most-confident candidate point; the four picked points are blended into one answer, weighted by how confident each heading was -- replaces the old contrast-sharpened averaging over the 1,024-tile scoreboard

stages.name: Confidence
stages.detail: confidence now comes from how tightly the four headings' picks agree with each other and how sure the winning pick was, instead of reading the shape of the old 1,024-tile scoreboard; still abstains below a tuned bar, still answers at least 40% of the time in every lighting condition

stages.name: Training data
stages.detail: every pass still draws a fresh 6,000 places per bucket, weighted so places whose map tile looks like some far-off tile are drawn roughly twice as often -- unchanged from round 35, just no longer sharing its map-grid constant with the model itself

stages.name: Training signal
stages.detail: instead of grading all 1,024 tiles at once, each training example only grades its single closest candidate point in full (a direct distance penalty) while the other 7 candidates get a small trickle of the same penalty so none of them go completely unused; a second signal teaches the confidence weights to point at whichever of the 8 candidates turned out closest

stages.name: Training schedule
stages.detail: unchanged — 24 fresh-draw passes with the cosine learning-rate glide kept from round 25

implementation_brief:
Edit model/model.py only for the architecture; edit model/train.py only for the mechanical GRID_K decoupling described below. Preserve the fixed contracts stated in model/model.py's module docstring: train.py's CLI is untouched, and export_onnx must still produce, per area, a 1x3x128x128 float32-in-[0,1] -> [[u, v, conf]] ONNX graph (u, v normalized map coords, conf in [0,1]) that passes pipeline/score.py's frozen gates -- do not change export_onnx or the estimate_position() wrapper.

In model/model.py:
1. Remove: GRID_K, TARGET_SIGMA_CELLS, DECODE_BETA, _grid_centers(), and (inside TinyLocNet) self.loc_logits, self.dark_logits, self.gate, self.cell_u/self.cell_v buffers. Keep: GOOD_ERR_UV, PRETRAINED_TRUNK_PATH, IMAGENET_MEAN/STD, _build_pretrained_trunk(), _c4_stack(), self.features, self.norm_mean/std, self.layout_squeeze (unchanged, still Conv2d(feat_ch, layout_ch=8, 1) producing the 512-d flattened layout code at 8x8 spatial), self.conf_head structure (rewire its inputs, see below), self.conf_shift buffer and its calibration contract (train.py's calibrate_conf_shift() reads model(xb)[:, 2] and writes model.conf_shift -- do not touch that function, it needs zero changes).
2. Add constants: K_HYP = 8, WTA_EPSILON = 0.05, VOTE_BETA = 3.0 (temperature for blending the 4 rotation-views' winning points by confidence -- same role DECODE_BETA played, renamed since the mechanism differs).
3. Add a registered buffer `anchors`, shape [K_HYP, 2], fixed (not nn.Parameter) values [[0.2,0.2],[0.5,0.2],[0.8,0.2],[0.2,0.5],[0.8,0.5],[0.2,0.8],[0.5,0.8],[0.8,0.8]] (u,v each in [0,1], the 8 non-center cells of a 3x3 grid) -- these exist ONLY to break symmetry at initialization so the 8 hypotheses start spread apart; nothing clamps a hypothesis to stay near its anchor during training.
4. Add self.hyp_head = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(feat_ch + layout_ch*fmap_hw*fmap_hw + 1, 128), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(128, K_HYP*3)) -- the '+1' is raw brightness (lum), folded in directly as an input feature since there is no more separate lighting-gate/dark-expert dispatch (exp 12's blended dual-head is retired: a single head now sees brightness as one more number, per the hypothesis's note that this is a structural consequence of dropping logit-space blending, not a second free experiment).
5. Rewrite TinyLocNet.forward(self, x, return_logits=False) as: compute lum [N,1] and x4/fmap/f4/layout4/lum4 exactly as the current code does (unchanged data flow through the trunk). Then: desc4 = torch.cat([f4, layout4, lum4], dim=1); raw = self.hyp_head(desc4).view(4*n, K_HYP, 3); hyp_uv = (self.anchors.unsqueeze(0) + raw[..., :2]).clamp(0.0, 1.0)  # [4n,K,2]; w_logits = raw[..., 2]  # [4n,K]; w = softmax(w_logits, dim=1); winner_idx = w.argmax(dim=1); winner_uv = hyp_uv.gather(1, winner_idx.view(-1,1,1).expand(-1,1,2)).squeeze(1)  # [4n,2]; winner_w = w.gather(1, winner_idx.view(-1,1)).squeeze(1)  # [4n]. Reshape block-major (matching the existing convention already used for f4/logits4, i.e. .view(4, n, -1), NOT interleaved -- _c4_stack's torch.cat([x,r90,r180,r270]) and lum.repeat(4,1) are both block-major, so every per-view reshape in this file must stay block-major): winner_uv4 = winner_uv.view(4, n, 2); winner_w4 = winner_w.view(4, n). view_weight = softmax(VOTE_BETA * winner_w4, dim=0).unsqueeze(-1)  # [4,n,1]; uv = (view_weight * winner_uv4).sum(dim=0)  # [n,2]. f_avg = f4.view(4, n, -1).mean(dim=0)  # [n,feat_ch], same role the old averaged 'f' played for conf_head. spread = winner_uv4.std(dim=0).sum(dim=1, keepdim=True)  # [n,1], detach it. mean_w = winner_w4.mean(dim=0).unsqueeze(-1)  # [n,1], detach it. conf_head input becomes torch.cat([f_avg.detach(), spread, mean_w], dim=1) -- update self.conf_head's first Linear's in_features from feat_ch+3 to feat_ch+2 accordingly (fewer scalar signals than before: peak/entropy/gap -> spread/mean_w). z = self.conf_head(...); conf = sigmoid(z - self.conf_shift); out = torch.cat([uv, conf], dim=1). If return_logits: return out, torch.cat([hyp_uv.reshape(4*n, K_HYP*2), w_logits], dim=1); else return out.
6. Rewrite loss_fn(pred, aux, target_uv): k = K_HYP; n4 = aux.shape[0]; hyp_uv = aux[:, :2*k].view(n4, k, 2); w_logits = aux[:, 2*k:]; target4 = target_uv.repeat(4, 1)  # block-major, matches aux's row order; dist2 = ((hyp_uv - target4.unsqueeze(1)) ** 2).sum(-1)  # [n4,k]; best_idx = dist2.argmin(dim=1); best_onehot = nn.functional.one_hot(best_idx, k).float(); wta_w = best_onehot + (WTA_EPSILON/(k-1)) * (1 - best_onehot); coord_loss = (wta_w * dist2).sum(dim=1).mean(); weight_ce = nn.functional.cross_entropy(w_logits, best_idx); coord_err = ((pred[:, :2] - target_uv) ** 2).sum(dim=1); with torch.no_grad(): good = (coord_err.sqrt() < GOOD_ERR_UV).float(); conf_loss = nn.functional.binary_cross_entropy(pred[:, 2].clamp(1e-6, 1-1e-6), good); return coord_loss + weight_ce + 0.3 * conf_loss.
7. Update the module docstring's running commentary (append an 'Exp 37' paragraph after the existing Exp 16 paragraph) describing this change in the same style as the existing entries -- do not delete or edit the Exp 1-16 paragraphs, they document real prior decisions.

In model/train.py (mechanical decoupling only, no behavior change): line 71's import currently reads `from model.model import GRID_K, build_model, export_onnx, loss_fn` -- change to `from model.model import build_model, export_onnx, loss_fn` and add a new module-level constant `CONFUSABILITY_GRID_K = 32` near the other constants (e.g. next to CONFUSABILITY_ALPHA). Replace the two remaining uses of `GRID_K` (in compute_cell_weights' `k = GRID_K` and train_area's crop_cell computation `... * GRID_K ...`) with `CONFUSABILITY_GRID_K`. No other lines in train.py change -- train_area's call `out, logits = model(xb, return_logits=True); loss = loss_fn(out, logits, yb)` (the loss_fn call site) and calibrate_conf_shift() both already work unchanged against the new forward()/loss_fn() signatures, since the (pred, aux, target_uv) contract shape ([N,3] pred, aux as a single 2-D tensor, [N,2] target) matches the existing (pred, logits, target_uv) call exactly.

After implementing: run a quick shape sanity check locally if possible (build_model(), forward a random [2,3,128,128] tensor, confirm output shape [2,3] and no NaNs) before handing off to the harness's training run -- the frozen scorer is your first real signal otherwise, and this experiment touches every internal shape in the head.
